title: How Trevor Noah Explained Happiness Better Than Most Self-Help Books
subtitle: Learning to find joy in the trenches

blocks.content: Welcome to Irrational Motivation! This is a series of articles I’ve been planning for quite a while now, and with enough ideas for a few articles, I feel like it’s a good time to launch.

The creation of this series was quite random. I have a notion page full of potential article ideas, which I populate every so often when an idea floats into my mind. Given this brainstorming process, what tends to happen is that the ideas I end up adding are often spur-of-the-moment thoughts sprinkled throughout the weeks. Some are added right after a shower, with my hair still wet. Some are added during lectures, with my professor still mid-sentence. And some are added at 2am.

Now, since many of the ideas are spur-of-the-moment, a good amount of them are based on certain emotions I feel at the time, rather than more logical thoughts. A few of them specifically are random things that I found in one way or another motivated me a lot, measured simply by how they made me feel in the moment.
Anyways, onto the first article.

You can’t connect the dots looking forward. You can only connect them looking backwards. So you have to trust that the dots will somehow connect in your future.

- Steve Jobs (2005 Stanford Commencement Address)
There’s a unique sense of motivation that I feel every time the dots connect. To see your thoughts, hypotheses, and acquired knowledge actually play out in real life.
One of these moments of connection would occur recently, when I was watching Trevor Noah’s newest special, Joy in the Trenches.
Around the 50-minute mark, he said
You know, the conundrum of life is that just because the big is happening doesn’t mean that the small stops.

Immediately, a connection formed in my mind. The third lesson I mentioned in the happiness documentary was about appreciating the small things in life to sustain happiness. Implicit in that was the idea to not focus as much on the big things, something we tend to do when it comes to thinking about happiness.
As if on cue, he rephrased his statement into a rhetorical question, perfectly capturing what I was thinking:
How much of the big do you focus on, and how much of the small do you pay any heed?

He then gave an example to illustrate what he meant. It was an online exchange between two people. Someone had just graduated with straight A’s, and they were posting about how happy they were. However, some people in the comments were reacting quite negatively to the post. They questioned why this person would even think of celebrating, especially at a time like this.

Now, my initial reaction to this story was to side with the recent graduate. Perhaps I am biased, given that I am also a student, but I honestly did not see why it was anyone’s business to put someone down for an accomplishment they recently had. Especially when their joy was in no way affecting the lives of these other people.
Trevor had a different response.

I’ll be honest with you. I could actually see where both people were coming from. Emotionally, I got it. On the one hand, someone was saying,

“How can you throw a celebration at a time when it feels like the world as we know it has fallen apart? How can you be joyous when it looks like every norm and institution we have understood that makes up the fabric of society is falling apart? Our public trust is falling apart. Our trust in each other is falling apart. How can you throw a party?”

But the other person was saying,

“How can I not? I have lived and worked my entire life to get to this place. I have finally achieved it against all odds, and so for this moment, I wish to enjoy.”

And both are right.

At this point, I was somewhat in disagreement with the statement. I did not think the people in the comments were right. However, the analogy he used, followed by the punchline of the story, made me understand why he framed it that way.

In every hospital around the world, there’s a ward where a baby has just been born, and another where somebody has just died. Jubilation at the start of a new life. Despair at the loss of someone you’ve always loved. Neither ward incorrect.

But then, which one do you focus on?

The merits of both responses were not the core of his point. This was accepted as something that people will always be justified to do. From the perspective of someone who has lived the life they lived, suffered through what they suffered, who exists as they are at that specific point in time, their response is correct to them.
The question is, what do you choose to focus on despite that?
This idea fits quite nicely with the second lesson on happiness that I talked about, which is that we have full control over our happiness. It is ultimately a choice we make.
Someone can have all the reasons to be sad. Go through the worst things imaginable. There’s nothing wrong with feeling negative about those things. In fact, it’s completely justified.
But again, the question is, what do you choose to focus on? What do you choose to feel?
And that is fundamentally a different discussion from how seemingly justified a response to something is.
Unlike Socrates, Trevor actually gave an answer to his deeply thought-provoking question. His answer came from a book. It was filled with accounts from soldiers who served during World War I.

A lot of them talk about the trench warfare, which was some of the worst warfare that man has ever invented. It went on for years and years and years. These soldiers would cycle in and out. Every day could’ve been their last. And in the midst of all of this despair, there’s one common thread that you see with every single soldier they speak to, almost every single one. And that is the fact that they found their joy in the trenches.
Trevor went on to describe some of these moments of joy.
Card games that they played.
Satirical newspapers that they started.

One of the wildest things I read was, there was a day when a cow walked into the battlefield. It was in no-man’s land. It was perfectly between the Allies and the Axis. So it was like both sides wanted the cow, and then they shouted across to the Germans.

They said, “We want the cow.”

The Germans were like, “No, ve vant ze cow.”

And then they agreed. They said, “Okay, let’s play a game. We’ll throw up coins in the air, and we’ll try to shoot them. Whoever shoots the coin gets to keep the cow.”

And then the English soldiers shot the coin, and then the Germans were like, “Okay, you win the cow, but we’d like our coins back.”

[So] the English soldier went, got the coins, and was like, “Oh, they’re going to kill me.”

They got the coins, gave them to the Germans, and then ran back and got into the trenches. And then everyone was like “fun game!”

As you can probably guess, one of my favourite things to do in my spare time is to watch comedy specials. I believe stand-up comedy is the highest form of communication that can be mastered. It requires not only an expert level of broad appeal and delivery in public speaking, but also a nuanced understanding of the dynamics in one-to-one conversations to get someone to laugh. One of the most powerful ways to perfect this combination is by telling a good story. This was a great example of how, since the endpoint these stories led to was a simple yet incredibly insightful answer.

They found their joy in the trenches. And it hit me because it made me realize, they remembered what they were fighting for. They were fighting to live. They weren’t fighting to fight. If you forget what you’re fighting for, then there’s no point in fighting. You’re fighting to be joyous. You’re fighting to explore yourself. You’re fighting to have adventures. You’re fighting to laugh. You don’t just fight to fight. You’re fighting to live.

Just because the big is happening, doesn’t mean that the small stops.

What I loved most was this circular ending. It was so masterfully done. The amount of added meaning to this single phrase was incredible. I remember physically feeling the difference that phrase felt in my mind the first time he said it, compared to the final time.

This difference in feeling, despite the words being the same, feeds into the first lesson I had for my happiness project, which is that the things that make us happy often seem very simple. But in reality, it is the deeply personal stories behind these simple things that generate happiness.

Much like this lesson suggests, the true meaning of this phrase is not located in the literal words, but rather the story and explanation behind it. Without the story, that phrase would hold the same significance as something you would see in a picture frame you bought at Walmart.

Being able to connect the lessons I had thought of about happiness with what Trevor was saying felt incredibly rewarding. I still remember when these ideas were merely sticky notes on my wall. A fragmented jumble of thoughts extracted from pages of journal entries filled with messy handwriting. They didn’t feel important at the time. They didn’t seem like ideas I would look back on. They were just random bits of consciousness that I stuck on my wall one summer afternoon. But their meaning developed over time. Gained significance when I began connecting these dots or even just seeing them as dots that could be connected. Slowly consolidating into important life lessons after repeatedly seeing them play out in real life.
And to see how all these dots have connected, how some random messy thoughts can be turned into what they are now, makes me irrationally motivated.
